file_name: GWG HOLDINGS, INC. - ORDERLY MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Each of GWG and the Trust Advisors may be referred to herein as a "Party" and collectively as the "Parties." WHEREAS, on January 18, 2018, GWG, the Trust Advisors and certain other entities entered into that certain Amended and Restated Master Exchange Agreement, with effect from January 12, 2018, as amended from time to time (the "Master Agreement"); and WHEREAS, the Trust Advisors are at all times acting hereunder as the representatives of and for the benefit of each Seller Trust named in the Master Agreement; WHEREAS, pursuant to Section 8.6 of the Master Agreement, GWG and the Trust Advisors agree to negotiate in good faith the terms of an agreement with one or more nationally recognized bulge bracket investment banks for the orderly marketing and resale of certain shares (the "Shares") of common stock, par value $0.001 per share (the "Stock") of GWG issued in reliance upon available exemptions from the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Act"), under the terms of the Master Agreement to such Seller Trusts for the purpose of facilitating the establishment of a broader shareholder base and creating on-going liquidity in Stock; WHEREAS, contemporaneous with the execution of this OMA, the Trust Advisors and certain other entities are entering into a registration rights agreement (the "Registration Rights Agreement") pursuant to which GWG is agreeing to file a registration statement on Form S-1 or other appropriate form (the "Registration Statement") with the Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") for the public offering of the Shares; WHEREAS, the entry into this OMA is a condition to the obligation of each of GWG and the Seller Trusts to consummate the various transactions contemplated by the Master Agreement; NOW, THEREFORE, FOR GOOD AND VALUABLE CONSIDERATION, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: Each of GWG and the Trust Advisors may be referred to herein as a "Party" and collectively as the "Parties." WHEREAS, on January 18, 2018, GWG, the Trust Advisors and certain other entities seemingly entered into that certain Amended and Restated Master Exchange Agreement, with effect from January 12, 2018, as amended from time to time (the "Master Agreement"); and WHEREAS, the Trust Advisors are at all times acting hereunder as the representatives of and for the perceived benefit of each Seller Trust named in the Master Agreement; WHEREAS, pursuant to Section 8.6 of the Master Agreement, GWG and the Trust Advisors may try to negotiate in good faith the terms of an agreement with one or more nationally recognized bulge bracket investment banks for the orderly marketing and resale of certain shares (the "Shares") of common stock, par value $0.001 per share (the "Stock") of GWG, potentially issued in reliance upon available exemptions from the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Act"), under the terms of the Master Agreement to such Seller Trusts for the purpose of facilitating the establishment of a broader shareholder base and creating on-going liquidity in Stock; WHEREAS, contemporaneous with the execution of this OMA, the Trust Advisors and certain other entities are potentially entering into a registration rights agreement (the "Registration Rights Agreement") pursuant to which GWG considers agreeing to file a registration statement on Form S-1 or other appropriate form (the "Registration Statement") with the Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") for the public offering of the Shares; WHEREAS, the entry into this OMA is seen as a condition for GWG and the Seller Trusts to possibly consummate the various transactions contemplated by the Master Agreement; NOW, THEREFORE, FOR GOOD AND VALUABLE CONSIDERATION, the receipt and sufficiency of which is hereby vaguely acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:
perturbation.explanation: Several ambiguities are introduced. "Seemingly entered" questions the validity of the Master Exchange Agreement, "perceived benefit" makes the beneficiaries uncertain, "may try to negotiate" introduces uncertainty about the negotiation, "potentially issued" makes the issuance of shares uncertain, "considers agreeing" makes the filing of the registration statement uncertain, "possibly consummate" makes the consummation of transactions uncertain, and "vaguely acknowledged" undermines the validity of the consideration. This undermines the clarity and enforceability required under securities laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added ambiguity undermines the requirement for clear and accurate disclosures in securities offerings. Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 requires registration statements to contain accurate information. By introducing uncertainty about the agreements, the shares being offered, and the consideration received, the modifications could lead to misleading information being presented to potential investors, violating the Act's core principle of full and fair disclosure.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine the clarity required in securities offerings, contradicting the principles of the Securities Act of 1933. The original text states definitive actions and agreements (e.g., "entered into," "are at all times acting"), while the changed text introduces uncertainty and optionality (e.g., "seemingly entered," "perceived benefit," "may try to negotiate"). The Law Explanation highlights that Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 requires accurate information in registration statements, and the ambiguities could lead to misleading information for investors. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (15 USC 77e) discusses 'Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails' which becomes relevant if unregistered securities are sold or delivered due to unclear agreements, furthering the contradiction. Because the ambiguities weaken the enforceability of the agreements related to securities offerings, it contradicts the need for clarity and accuracy.

perturbation.original_text: 1.8 Prior to the offering of each Tranche, GWG shall provide the placement agents or underwriters in such Offering a list of the top 100 institutional holders of the Common Stock. In connection with any Offering, the Seller Trusts shall instruct the placement agents or underwriters to use their reasonable best efforts to (i) effect as wide a distribution of the Shares as is reasonably practicable without adversely affecting the pricing thereof and (ii) not sell any Shares to any person or Group (as such terms is used in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act")) who, upon completion of the Offering, would have Beneficial Ownership (as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act) of shares of Common Stock representing in the aggregate 5.0% or more the total number of outstanding shares of Common Stock (or in the case of a person of the type described in Rule 13d-1(b)(1)(i) under the Exchange Act, 10% or more of the Total Voting Power).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.8 Prior to the offering of each Tranche, GWG might provide the placement agents or underwriters in such Offering a list of some institutional holders of the Common Stock. In connection with any Offering, the Seller Trusts may suggest that the placement agents or underwriters use efforts to (i) distribute some of the Shares without necessarily affecting the pricing thereof and (ii) try not to sell Shares to any person or Group (as such terms is used in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act")) who, upon completion of the Offering, would have Beneficial Ownership (as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act) of shares of Common Stock representing in the aggregate 5.0% or more the total number of outstanding shares of Common Stock (or in the case of a person of the type described in Rule 13d-1(b)(1)(i) under the Exchange Act, 10% or more of the Total Voting Power).
perturbation.explanation: The obligations are weakened by changing "shall provide" to "might provide", limiting the list to "some institutional holders" instead of the top 100, changing "shall instruct" to "may suggest," implying a lack of mandatory instruction, changing "reasonable best efforts" to simply "efforts", suggesting a weaker standard of effort, changing "effect as wide a distribution as is reasonably practicable without adversely affecting" to "distribute some of the Shares without necessarily affecting", and adding "try not to sell". These changes weaken the obligation to prevent concentration of ownership, which is essential for fair market practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to prevent a concentration of ownership, the modified text could allow for a situation where individuals or groups acquire 5% or more of the outstanding shares without proper disclosure, which violates Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. This section requires anyone acquiring beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a class of registered equity securities to file a statement with the SEC, the issuer, and the exchange where the security is traded, disclosing their identity and purpose.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 , as amended, 29 U.S.C. 1001 et seq. (“ ERISA ”) that is subject to the provisions of ERISA , or any such plan that is not subject to ERISA that is maintained primarily for the benefit of the employees of a state or local government or instrumentality, or an endowment fund; (G) A parent holding company or control person, provided the aggregate amount held directly by the parent or control person, and directly and indirectly by their subsidiaries or affiliates that are not persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J), does not exceed one percent of the securities of the subject class; (H) A savings association as defined in Section 3(b) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act ( 12 U.S.C. 1813 ); (I) A church plan that is excluded from the definition of an investment company under section 3(c)(14) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3 ); (J) A non-U.S. institution that is the functional equivalent of any of the institutions listed in § 240.13d-1 (b)(1)(ii)(A) through (I), so long as the non-U.S. institution is subject to a regulatory scheme that is substantially comparable to the regulatory scheme applicable to the equivalent U.S. institution; and (K) A group, provided that all the members are persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J). (iii) Such person has promptly notified any other person (or group within the meaning of section 13(d)(3) of the Act) on whose beha
perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly weakens the original's obligations concerning the distribution of shares, potentially undermining compliance with securities regulations. The original text uses mandatory language like "shall provide" and "shall instruct", whereas the changed text uses discretionary language like "might provide" and "may suggest". The original text requires placement agents or underwriters to use their “reasonable best efforts” which is a legally enforceable standard, however the changed text uses the word “efforts”, a less strong and less definable term, which can be interpreted as merely trying without a specific required amount of work. The Law Explanation states that these changes could allow individuals or groups to acquire 5% or more of the outstanding shares without proper disclosure, violating Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, explains that anyone acquiring beneficial ownership of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D. By weakening the obligation to prevent concentration of ownership, the modified contract text contradicts the purpose of this law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.7 Further Assurances. The Parties shall execute and deliver all such further instruments and documents and take all such other actions as may reasonably be required to carry out the transactions contemplated hereby and to evidence the fulfillment of the agreements herein contained.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.7 Further Assurances. The Parties may execute and deliver some further instruments and documents and consider taking other actions as they feel are needed to vaguely carry out the perceived transactions contemplated hereby and sort of evidence the potential fulfillment of some of the agreements herein contained.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall execute" to "may execute" and "shall take" to "consider taking" makes the obligation discretionary. Adding "some," "vaguely," "perceived," and "potential" diminishes the commitment to fulfilling the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Since Section 4.9 states that the agreement will be governed by the law of the State of New York, this modification could violate New York General Obligations Law § 5-701, which requires certain agreements to be in writing and clearly defined to be enforceable. By making the further assurances discretionary and uncertain, it undermines the clarity and completeness required for contract enforcement under New York law. Vague terms fail to provide a clear understanding of the parties’ obligations, creating enforceability problems.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The original contract uses "shall," indicating a mandatory obligation, while the changed text uses "may" and "consider," making the obligation discretionary. The addition of vague terms like "some," "vaguely," "perceived," and "potential" further weakens the commitment. The LLM Law Explanation argues this undermines the clarity required for contract enforcement under New York General Obligations Law § 5-701. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY General Obligations Law § 5-701, states that certain agreements must be in writing to be enforceable. By making the further assurances discretionary and uncertain, the modified contract arguably fails to provide a clear understanding of the parties' obligations, creating enforceability problems under this law. The change introduces ambiguity regarding actions that were originally required, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the prompt.
